context.setting: Watching TV with TV in Front

episode_text:
day=11; meal=bite-sized pizza and broccoli; setting=Watching TV with TV in Front; time_of_day=evening;
preferences: microwave_time=3 min; occlusion_relevance=minimize front occlusion; robot_speed=medium; skewering_axis=perpendicular to major axis; web_interface_confirmation=no; transfer_mode=inside mouth transfer; outside_mouth_distance=not applicable; convey_robot_ready_for_initiating_transfer=LED; detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfer_feeding=button; detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfer_drinking=button; detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfer_wiping=button; convey_robot_ready_for_completing_transfer=LED; detect_user_completed_transfer_feeding=perception; detect_user_completed_transfer_drinking=button; detect_user_completed_transfer_wiping=button; retract_between_bites=yes; bite_dipping_preference=do not dip; wait_before_autocontinue_seconds=1000 sec

microwave_time.user_tendencies: IF food is a cold-served item such as fruit or dessert AND any dining context AND any affective state THEN no microwave BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected no microwave for cold meals, preserving intended serving temperature. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special temperature constraint THEN 2 min BECAUSE this remains the best-supported default for typical warm meals. IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 2 min BECAUSE the user selected standard reheating in that social context. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm snack meal THEN heating preference is mixed, with both 1 min and 3 min observed, so 2 min is the most stable front-TV summary BECAUSE the new front-TV pizza meal shows the user does not consistently want lighter reheating in that context. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 2 min BECAUSE the user selected standard reheating for a warm TV meal on the right. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 2 min BECAUSE the user also selected standard reheating in that media context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 1 min BECAUSE the user selected lighter reheating for one left-work warm meal. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 3 min BECAUSE the user selected the hottest reheating level in that right-work meal, showing work-context temperature preference can vary by setup.

occlusion_relevance.user_tendencies: IF dining context includes a person on the right OR a TV/device on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN minimize right occlusion BECAUSE the user repeatedly chose to avoid blocking salient right-side social or visual targets. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN minimize front occlusion BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected front-occlusion minimization when the salient target was directly ahead, including the new front-TV meal. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE the user did not request left-side occlusion minimization in that media context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit meal THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE the user repeatedly declined occlusion-based adjustment in left-side work contexts. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE no evidence shows occlusion avoidance is needed without a salient social or media target.

robot_speed.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed in that social setting, reinforcing medium as a comfortable socially appropriate pace. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a light cold item or a warm snack/entree THEN speed preference is mixed between fast and medium, with the newer front-TV warm meal selecting medium, so medium is now the more conservative front-TV summary BECAUSE the user does not always prioritize maximum speed when watching TV ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed in that right-TV meal. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed in that context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN fast BECAUSE the user selected fast speed while working, indicating that some task-focused meals prioritize efficiency. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed for fruit while working, suggesting the work-context preference for speed is moderated by meal type. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN medium BECAUSE this remains the best-supported comfortable default.

transfer_mode.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN outside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user selected the less intrusive transfer style in that social setting. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected direct delivery in front-TV contexts, indicating a stable preference to reduce the need to lean forward while attending to media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN outside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user selected the less intrusive, more user-controlled style in that context. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user selected inside-mouth transfer in that media context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit meal THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected direct delivery while working, showing that active device engagement favors reduced user movement. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user also selected direct delivery in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN outside mouth transfer BECAUSE this remains the conservative default and is physically feasible for the user.

outside_mouth_distance.user_tendencies: IF transfer mode is outside mouth transfer AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected a moderate stopping distance that balances comfort and reachability, including in both social and right-TV outside-mouth meals. IF transfer mode is inside mouth transfer AND any dining context AND any affective state AND any food condition THEN not applicable BECAUSE this parameter only applies to outside-mouth transfer.

convey_robot_ready_for_initiating_transf.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE the user selected a quiet visual cue in that social setting. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN front-TV cue preference is mixed between no cue and LED, with repeated evidence favoring reduced interruption but the new meal showing acceptance of a quiet visual cue, so LED is the best-supported conservative summary BECAUSE it preserves low disruption without fully suppressing readiness information. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the user selected the most explicit multimodal cue in that right-TV meal, suggesting stronger notification is preferred when attention is directed to a right-side screen. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE the user selected LED rather than suppressing cues. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN no cue BECAUSE the user selected no cue while working on a warm entree, indicating a preference to suppress readiness notifications during some task-focused meals. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN speech BECAUSE the user selected speech in the same work context for fruit, showing that cue preference can shift with meal type. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the user selected multimodal cueing in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE this remains the conservative low-burden default outside observed task-focused suppression or stronger right-side multimodal cueing.

detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfe.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user repeatedly chose button-based initiation in social meals and can comfortably press buttons, consistent with avoiding ambiguity from open-mouth signals during conversation. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN front-TV preference is mixed between autocontinue and button, with the new meal showing explicit control, so button is now the more cautious front-TV summary BECAUSE the user may want deliberate timing even while attending to media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user preferred low-effort perceptual initiation for feeding in that context rather than manual confirmation. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user selected manual readiness signaling in that side-TV context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the user selected automatic initiation while working, suggesting task-focused contexts can shift preference from explicit control toward reduced interaction burden. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation for feeding in that work-and-fruit context, showing that for feeding the user may accept low-effort perceptual initiation rather than button press when the meal is light. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation in that right-side work context.

IF dining context is social AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user repeatedly preferred manual readiness signaling for drinking in social settings and can comfortably press buttons. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the new front-TV meal selected explicit manual readiness, weakening the earlier autocontinue pattern and making button the more stable summary for drinking. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN button BECAUSE the user selected explicit manual readiness signaling in that right-TV meal. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected manual readiness signaling, reinforcing a side-TV preference for explicit control. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN evidence is mixed between autocontinue and button, with the specific fruit meal favoring button, so button is the more conservative summary for drinking in left-work contexts BECAUSE the user often prefers deliberate sip timing. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN button BECAUSE the user selected explicit manual readiness for drinking in that specific work-and-fruit context, indicating more deliberate control for sip timing than for feeding. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation for drinking in that right-side work context.

IF dining context is social AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user repeatedly preferred manual readiness signaling for wiping in social settings and can comfortably press buttons. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the new front-TV meal selected explicit manual readiness, weakening the earlier autocontinue pattern and making button the more stable summary for wiping. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN button BECAUSE the user selected explicit manual readiness signaling in that right-TV meal. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected manual readiness signaling, reinforcing a side-TV preference for explicit control. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the user selected automatic initiation while working, extending the pattern that task-focused contexts can favor lower-effort interaction. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN button BECAUSE the user selected explicit manual readiness for wiping in that specific work-and-fruit context, indicating more deliberate control for wiping timing than the earlier work default suggested. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation in that right-side work context.

convey_robot_ready_for_completing_transf.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE the user selected a quiet visual completion cue in that social setting. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN front-TV completion cue preference is mixed between no cue and LED, with the new meal showing acceptance of a quiet visual cue, so LED is the best-supported conservative summary BECAUSE it preserves low interruption while still marking transfer completion. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the user selected multimodal completion cueing in that right-TV meal, suggesting stronger notification is preferred when attention is directed to a right-side screen. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE the user selected LED in that media context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN no cue BECAUSE the user selected no cue while working, indicating a preference to suppress completion notifications during some attention-demanding meals. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN speech BECAUSE the user selected speech in that work-and-fruit context, showing that completion cue preference can vary with meal type. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the user selected multimodal completion cueing in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE this remains the conservative low-burden default outside observed task-focused suppression or stronger right-side multimodal cueing.

detect_user_completed_transfer_drinking.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user repeatedly chose button-based completion for drinking in social meals, favoring explicit control over automatic inference. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN evidence is mixed between autocontinue and button, with the new front-TV meal selecting button, so button is now the more stable front-TV summary BECAUSE the user may want explicit control over sip completion even when attention is directed to media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN button BECAUSE the user preferred explicit button-based completion there. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected button-based completion, reinforcing a side-TV preference for explicit control. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN button BECAUSE the user kept explicit button-based completion while working, suggesting that even when initiation is automated the user prefers deliberate completion control for drinking. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected button-based completion, strengthening the pattern that drinking completion remains explicitly user-controlled in left-side work contexts. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the user selected automatic completion in that right-side work context, showing that work can shift drinking completion toward lower-effort automation.

detect_user_completed_transfer_wiping.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user repeatedly chose button-based completion for wiping in social meals, favoring explicit control over automatic inference. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN evidence is mixed between autocontinue and button, with the new front-TV meal selecting button, so button is now the more stable front-TV summary BECAUSE the user may want explicit control over wipe completion even when attention is directed to media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN button BECAUSE the user preferred explicit button-based completion there. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected button-based completion, reinforcing a side-TV preference for explicit control. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN button BECAUSE the user kept explicit button-based completion while working, suggesting that even when initiation is automated the user prefers deliberate completion control for wiping. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected button-based completion, strengthening the pattern that wiping completion remains explicitly user-controlled in left-side work contexts. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the user selected automatic completion in that right-side work context, showing that work can shift wiping completion toward lower-effort automation.

retract_between_bites.user_tendencies: IF dining context includes a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN yes BECAUSE the user chose to reduce prolonged obstruction in that right-side social context, even at the cost of longer meal time. IF dining context includes a TV/device on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN no BECAUSE the user did not want extra retract motions there, indicating that right-side visual relevance alone does not always justify the time cost of retraction. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN preference is mixed, with earlier no selections but the new front-TV meal selecting yes, so front-TV meals show context sensitivity rather than a single stable rule BECAUSE the user sometimes accepts longer meals to reduce obstruction when the salient target is directly ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN no BECAUSE the user also skipped retraction in that context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN no BECAUSE the user skipped retraction while working on that meal, reinforcing a general preference to avoid extra time costs in many work contexts. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN yes BECAUSE the user selected retraction in that specific work-and-fruit context, showing that when working the user may sometimes trade efficiency for reduced obstruction depending on the meal.

bite_dipping_preference.user_tendencies: IF food includes a dip or sauce AND dining context is social AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN less BECAUSE the user selected reduced dipping for a social meal with sauce, consistent with limiting messiness. IF food is a typical warm entree without user-selected dipping behavior AND dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user selected no dipping for a social warm entree. IF food is a warm meal served with a dip AND dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user selected no dipping for a front-TV meal with ranch dressing, and the new front-TV pizza-and-broccoli meal again selected no dipping, reinforcing that media-focused meals do not prompt dipping. IF food is a cold dessert or fruit with topping rather than a dippable sauce AND any dining context AND any affective state THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user selected no dipping for strawberries with whipped cream and again for a fruit plate, indicating these foods were not treated as dipping targets. IF food is a typical sauced entree without user-selected dipping behavior AND dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user did not request dipping for the meal. IF food is a typical sauced entree without user-selected dipping behavior AND dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user again declined dipping for a sauced warm entree in that media context. IF food is a typical sauced entree without user-selected dipping behavior AND dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user again declined dipping for a sauced entree, reinforcing that sauce present in the dish does not by itself create a dipping preference. IF food is a typical entree served with a dip AND dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user selected no dipping for chicken nuggets with ketchup, showing that even clearly dippable foods are not necessarily preferred for dipping in work contexts. IF food has no dippable item or sauce AND any dining context AND any affective state THEN do not dip BECAUSE dipping is not relevant.

wait_before_autocontinue_seconds.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN 100 sec BECAUSE the user selected the middle wait time in a social meal, consistent with allowing time for explicit user control without making automation excessively delayed. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN wait preference is mixed between 10 sec and 1000 sec, with the new front-TV meal selecting the longest delay, so 1000 sec is the better summary when the user also chooses more explicit control in front-TV contexts BECAUSE the user may want substantial time before any automatic progression while focused on media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 100 sec BECAUSE the user preferred a moderate wait there, indicating that right-side TV does not always call for the very long timeout seen in another side-TV case. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN 1000 sec BECAUSE the user selected a very long wait, suggesting that some side-TV contexts call for more time before automatic progression. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 10 sec BECAUSE the user selected a very short wait while working, extending the pattern that task-focused contexts can favor quick automatic progression. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN 1000 sec BECAUSE the user selected a very long wait in that work-and-fruit context, showing that food/context combinations can reverse the earlier work-context tendency toward short waits. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 10 sec BECAUSE the user selected a very short wait in that right-side work context, reinforcing that many active work meals favor quick automatic progression. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN 100 sec BECAUSE this remains the conservative default absent broader evidence.
